speaker: Leo Guilfoy
words: He didn't have much of an education, but he was smart. I think I told you that one about the warehouses. They used to come out here, you know, some people, the headquarters in St Paul, and they'd come out here and they'd get acquainted with the men, call them by their first name. Well, when a bunch of the young warehouses come out here, old Bill Dery, he was superintendent of the whole, oh, they had quite a few camps to pull out here around Bovill and all around that country. So he showed them a good time out in the logging camps and they said, well, Bill, now believe me, when you come back, we'll get you back in St Paul and we'll show you a good time. Well, they got him cornered up there in St Paul at a great big ball and they got him sidetracked in the reception room with a countess. They got him in there, they introduced him to the countess.

speaker: Leo Guilfoy
words: He looked up and down and he says, so you're a countess?

speaker: Leo Guilfoy
words: Yes, she said, I'm a countess. Boy, he says, you're the first countess I ever saw, but you, you're some chicken. And they were peeking in, listening to him on the door.

speaker: Leo Guilfoy
words: They asked the countess after, they said, what did you think of old lumberjack?

speaker: Leo Guilfoy
words: Oh, she said, I thought he was wonderful. There's one thing about him, he tells you what he thinks.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Is that the kind of guy that was in the area of Washington?

speaker: Leo Guilfoy
words: Oh yeah, you bet.

speaker: Leo Guilfoy
words: It's like, there was a train down there from Bovill down to Palouse and all the towns had a college name on them. All those places with this stuff had a college name. And then people go in there and they had some branches, you know, and nice chickens and all that. And one of the deeper ways is, you can see Bill Helmer, he was the headquarters there in Bovill, and they said some of these farmers are kicking because their eggs are getting broken, they said, and they weren't paid for them. Well, Bill says, pay them, he said. Sure, pay them, he said. Chickens are scratching their asses every day to lay more, what a weeker.

speaker: Leo Guilfoy
words: And they give him a secretary, girl secretary. Oh, Bill said, the letter came in, you know. All right, the letter he said to her. Here, take this down. I have you a letter of the 14th before me, and pretty soon it'll be behind me. He makes a lot of noise. That's the way he was.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, he's the man that bought up a lot of that timber for them to start with.

speaker: Leo Guilfoy
words: Yeah, him and old Bill Helmer blew in there in the early days. They'd cruise that timber and bought it, all around there, Elk River and all around that country. And they'd go out, old Bill would go out in the morning with a compassman, he'd be gone all day and maybe it'd snow, and then they'd shit in the snow and build a great big fire, and the heat would hit the snow and it'd keep him warm. He'd be gone all day, you know. This Bill Helmer. Yeah, he was quite a cruiser. I think I told you about them when he had a guy with him, a compassman with him, his name was Eddie Erickson. And they were about five miles from town, Eddie was quite a drinking guy. And he walked into town, you know, and Bill was staying out in the woods there, sleeping right on the floor, and the bacon was stuck under the mattress so the bear wouldn't get it. And late at night, somebody was shooing under the mattress, and Bill was sleeping there and the bear thought it was Eddie Erickson coming back drunk. He said, get in bed, you are drunk. And we had all of the bear. I thought, Eddie Erickson. I just put the bear in bed with him.

speaker: Leo Guilfoy
words: He told me he wasn't scared of bears, they're the big game. No, he couldn't. But Bill, along with him there, he gave me either compass, no, I had to go in a straight line so that he could work that side from me, where he was at.

speaker: Leo Guilfoy
words: And there was a bear with two cubs, the tree was a little slow, but one of the cubs was up a little ways, and I stopped and Bill said, where's Mary?

speaker: Leo Guilfoy
words: Well, I said, I'm not going by that thing. Oh, hell, he said, give me the compass. And he came over and got it from me. And he walked right by,

speaker: Leo Guilfoy
words: I said, keep on going, talking to him. He said, lady, you don't get them cubs out of the way, they're going to have something happen, I'm part of the last of the shooting. Talking to the birds as he went by, of course, I got up there quite a ways, and then I took the compass off and he went back where he belonged, sizing those trees up, see. And he was a good cruiser.

speaker: Leo Guilfoy
words: You bet.

speaker: Leo Guilfoy
words: Wonderful cruiser.

speaker: Leo Guilfoy
words: Do you think it was that the animals knew that...

speaker: Leo Guilfoy
words: Well, I was told tell you here about, I saw an old cedar maker. You know, the old cedar maker would go out in the woods and they'd get a strip, and the guy would show them what's their strip, all the timber on there, you'd make that. And this old guy told me, I went out to check some of the poles, and he told me, he said, I can put my hand on the, ah, you can't do that. Now, I looked, stuck around there quite a while, a couple of hours, and he stopped, and he did touch it. The deer shook, you know, all over, and touched it.

speaker: Leo Guilfoy
words: Well, I don't know whether the deer, well, you know, there's some horses that can trust you and won't trust her. And a dog like you, he hates somebody else for no reason whatsoever. They have a second sight on people. Look at the fish. Look at these salmon, steelhead. They go out, tiny, that big. They leave the river and go down, miles down the river, out in the ocean for four years. And they come back to where they were spawned. Now, they, nobody ever had any word what they do with that, but lately they're thinking they smell the water. Oh, boy, that, that, that salmon, that's something. They go up all these salmon, now, they have taken, now they come in, going up to Columbia, you know, Portland, and come on up here, and come up, all these little creeks in the place up here. Now, they picked one up before it spawned, and trucked it right across the land, right, and put it back in the ocean. That's all they were going to have it marked, and that'll suddenly come back, right back, and go back up the same river. Don't go up one of these rivers, just probably put it in. Go back down the river into the ocean, then down round and back up the mouth of the Columbia, you know. Look at all the creeks and streams that flows into that Columbia, and all these little tributaries of Columbia that close in. Well, they know just where they're going.

speaker: Leo Guilfoy
words: And then, and, and they're poor damn things, you know, they spawn, and the buck salmon goes with them, and, and when, when they spawn, then the buck goes ahead of them, and he spawn, and, and he covers the eggs with his spawn, see. And then they both go and die. They don't get back to the ocean, they die, right away. Sat down the river.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, yeah. It's incredible.

speaker: Leo Guilfoy
words: What?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: It's incredible.

speaker: Leo Guilfoy
words: It is. Now, they talk about pigeons and things like that, find a way, but that salmon is it. And you know, in, in their absence, little tiny fish that big, and in their absence, a lot of those creek mouths and beds have changed. Probably the dam in the four years, the dam went right up.

speaker: Leo Guilfoy
words: How do they know? How?

speaker: Leo Guilfoy
words: Now, they claim now, they say, they say, smell the water, before they just set instinct and all that. But right now, and all the way up, fishermen are trying to catch them, and animals, the coyote, the birds, trying to get them all the way up the river. And the jelly pickers where they spawn is a small place, not very deep, and lots of loose gravel on it. And the male fish makes a hole with the tail and puts the sperm in there, and the female goes, the male goes above it, and then when he puts his sperm right in there, it covers that hole with, and for every thousand that's hatched from the egg, there's only ten percent of them ever survive, to get back to the ocean. Look at all the dams all the way down. Look at all the commercial fishermen all the way down. I got a kid here, the grandson here, he fishes for salmon right along, steelhead.

speaker: Leo Guilfoy
words: Yes, it is, it's incredible, the return of the salmon. Gee, that's something they can't figure out.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You think that Bill Helmer had a, just had a real special instinct for working in the woods?

speaker: Leo Guilfoy
words: Well, they get that way, yeah. They get that way. Yeah.

speaker: Leo Guilfoy
words: They get that way, you know, and the trees is his, and the white pine tree, in those days, the white pine tree was it. And I used to go out and cut all my wood, you know, but I cut tamerac and red, tamerac and red fir, that was the best firewood. In those days, everything was, but you couldn't touch white pine, you could touch everything else without white pine.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: It's too valuable?

speaker: Leo Guilfoy
words: Yeah, it was the valuable it was then, but of course now it's sky high, now cedar's sky high, because they make this beautiful cedar siding, you know. I was talking with Saul Millman in Clarkson a long time ago, and he said, you were telling your dad that cedar wasn't near the price of white pine, he said, I'll just, I'll show you. And cedar now is a little above white pine, because it's the paneling they've got today. Beautiful cedar paneling, you know, all over the house. But the white pine is, years ago white pine was the lumber in the house. They'd make cabinets and all that, built -ins out of white pine. And it's a light, it's a nice colored wood, and easy to work. Tamerac is almost semi -hardwood.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was there much white pine disease when you were in the woods then, or has it just gotten much, much worse?

speaker: Leo Guilfoy
words: Oh, it's getting much, much worse.

speaker: Leo Guilfoy
words: You know, let's say 1920, then they realized how bad it was getting. And they don't, so far they don't know how to curtail it. They don't know how to, they thought some of these gooseberry bushes and things like that, which is right. The pollen from them would go to the white tree and then back to the white pine, and then they'd start in.

speaker: Leo Guilfoy
words: And they called it blister rust.

speaker: Leo Guilfoy
words: Now, they won't plant white pine anymore, because they can't control it. They can't control the disease. It just came from Europe to Canada, and then down here.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did the East Coast and Maine rust?

speaker: Leo Guilfoy
words: Yes, they called it, well, I think they've always called it white pine blister. But it went, no, it came to Canada, and then up across Canada, then came down this way. By that time, when it got here in 2021, most of all the white pine, good virgin white pine was in Maine, but that was mostly all cut tree. Now, the forestry, I think, has quit planting white pine. Now, there was some German, and he was up here, a place called Pierce, up here, head close, and he brought a certain kind of bug from Germany, and oh, they thought they had it, but it didn't work out. This bug would kill the white pine blister, but it didn't work out so good.

speaker: Leo Guilfoy
words: I remember this story that you told me about Bill Helmer and the cook.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you know that happened again?

speaker: Leo Guilfoy
words: Well, I got it, no, yeah. Well, anyhow, Bill was a guy, he was 100 percent potlatch.

speaker: Leo Guilfoy
words: You would ask him, he kept everything secret, see? And well, you'd ask him, well, you'd ask him, how long will it be out? Oh, which way are we going?

speaker: Leo Guilfoy
words: We're going to know it. That's all.

speaker: Leo Guilfoy
words: How long will it be?

speaker: Leo Guilfoy
words: Oh, a week or so, maybe. Well, anyhow, he started out, I wasn't with the men, he started out, he took about eight men, and he was going to run around a bunch of timber. He had the men to blaze trails so you could go around,

speaker: Leo Guilfoy
words: and in all of it, all of the homesteaders' place would just shoot him to camp. The homesteaders was gone. And so he told them which way they were going, and they got two wagons loaded with great big horses, you know, loaded with groceries. A guy named Frank Marbury loaded them, and he said, I'll tell you a joke, Frank played on them after that.

speaker: Leo Guilfoy
words: And the cook said, Bill, how long are you going?

speaker: Leo Guilfoy
words: Oh, Bill would tell them, oh, maybe a week. Well, I'll tell you this other story first before I tell you the one I was going to tell you. Well, Frank had loaded up, you know, and apparently he'd be gone only a couple of days, he'd have to go up there for a week.

speaker: Leo Guilfoy
words: Longer than that.

speaker: Leo Guilfoy
words: So Frank got mad at him, and more after that, one time he said, Frank, he said, Bill, how long are we going?

speaker: Leo Guilfoy
words: Oh, we'll be gone about, oh, a week or two. Well, Frank loaded up enough for a month. He got out there, and he was only there three days. All this stuff back, you know, and they fed good, see. There was nothing they couldn't order, and they fed good. But anyhow, this time about that, he was an old Dutchman, and he was a good camp cook, wonderful. And Bill always tried to get him. Well, this time Bill had hired him and about eight men to go out, about seven or eight men. Well, when he got out to this camp, he set the men all morning working on it, fixing everything up for the cook, and the people fixed them all up. They brought a nice big stove with them. And he said, now Dutch, he said, I've done everything I can for you. If you want me, I'll leave you a man here. He said, we've got all your wood cut and everything else cut, and we've got all the groceries in that root house over there, good -sized root house.

speaker: Leo Guilfoy
words: Well, Dutch says, no, he says, when will you be back?

speaker: Leo Guilfoy
words: Well, he said, we'll be back about six o 'clock. Well, Dutch says, everything will be ready. And he was a good cook, and he had everything to cook with. Well, when they got through out there, they come in all tired out, you know. Some of the men rushed to the kitchen, and they come back and said, Bill, the stove ain't been lit. He said, there's something wrong. He says, Dutch isn't there. Well, Bill told them, oh, now scatter out and track them, see if you can track them. Now, this might be serious, to carry them all around there. So they got tracking all around. Well, two went over to the root house, and pretty much went home in the root house. There's a bird in here. There's a bird in here. But all along, that's the funniest thing.

speaker: Leo Guilfoy
words: Well, Dutch, what do you want to take a bird in there with you for?

speaker: Leo Guilfoy
words: I didn't tell you, man. I was in here when I came in. And so they went and told Bill, he's in there with a bird.

speaker: Leo Guilfoy
words: What the hell is industrial doing there?

speaker: Leo Guilfoy
words: He left them there. So he told the man, he spotted a tree there. He knew the door opened in, see. He'd been around there before. So he got them just this little dead cedar, a big long pole, you know. And now he said, we'll push that out and let the bird out. Well, they got it there to push, and the bird got his front part out, but not his hind part, his hips. He was tearing up his legs. Well, then they loved him. Hey, Dutch, let go of his tail so he can get out. And they couldn't push the door. They watched the Bill with the song that they were. And they hollered, let go of his tail. I'm not holding his tail. I'm in the corner way over him. I'm not holding his tail away from him. Now, he'd been in there about four hours, I guess, with that bird. And he couldn't go forward because the door, the bird guarded the door. He wanted to get out. And as soon as he got there, well, he told him, he gave it to the man. Now he said, you take a double bidded axe to peace and stand right there. Now that bird comes out, he's going to jump us. He said, you know what to do when he starts out after us. And so they pushed it, you know, once more and the bird got out. And then they killed it all.

speaker: Leo Guilfoy
words: They said, hey, Dutch, where's his tail?

speaker: Leo Guilfoy
words: He didn't get a tail on him.

speaker: Leo Guilfoy
words: Where's his tail, Dutch?

speaker: Leo Guilfoy
words: And Dutch got in and he's going home. Long way he's going to help. Oh, Bill said, now wait a minute, Dutch, you're not going home. He said, not your fault, he said. And he says, I'll stop these guys kidding you about pulling the tail off the bird. And he went back and Bill sent all the men over there to the kitchen to help him fix things up, you know. Everything was wonderful. And then on after that, Dutch, when he opened his door, he looked to see where the bird was in there. He told Bill, he said, if he's in there, he's going to stay in there because I'm not going to go in with him. And they stayed out there, oh, I don't know, a couple of weeks maybe. And boy, he could travel in the woods all day long.

speaker: Leo Guilfoy
words: You see the way he dressed there with a compass in his hand?

speaker: Leo Guilfoy
words: Boy, could he travel in the woods. Great big hat and he'd dodge under this brush, you know. This time I was out with him, you know, and God, it's getting dark, you know. By God, Bill, I said, we lost. And he was going through a dense brush and it was getting dark. And we broke him up into a clearing. He looked, oh, he said, we may not have lost, there's the moon. There's the moon, we may not have lost.

speaker: Leo Guilfoy
words: But he, now, I've been with him sometimes looking for a corner, maybe 20 years ago that had been established. They'd try and put a witness tree and chop notches in the trees around it. By God, he said it should be, 20 years or more, it should be around here somewhere and help me look. Well, I wasn't much good at looking for it. By God, he said, now, he looked at that tree and I went to blemish on it, I could see. He took his axe and he chopped it out and there it was. Because it covered it over, see, the growth had covered it over. And then he found where the corner was. That corner was established and used to use them old corners. He remembered from when he'd been there before. Yeah, he remembered 20 years ago, longer than that. He remembered being there 20 years ago. Probably that same day it'd been 15 or 20 corners. But he remembered right there, he said there was a small white fur, it was a big white fur then growing up in 20 years.

speaker: Leo Guilfoy
words: I suppose if he hadn't been a timber cruiser, maybe 20, 30 years before, he could have been a trailblazer or a trapper. Oh, yeah, sure. Well, he wasn't a trapper. He worked around timber all his life. I wonder if he ever got lost.

speaker: Leo Guilfoy
words: No, but his brother did and that was my wife's dad. And his brother was better than he was. Well, of course, this story I'm going to tell you now wasn't mainly anything but Laid -Out County. But anyhow, her dad, his name was John Helmer. And he was an iron man, you know, he was one of the five iron men in Minnesota. And he was cruisers and everything else with. And he was going to look for some gold. And he took a geologist, that is, a university geologist, wasn't a geologist, but he was doing minerals and stuff like that. And it was right in the middle of July, hot. And he took him up to some lakes up in Canada and they had two canoes, one that him and his geologist were sitting in and a great big son was going to trail it behind with a grub and all that stuff in it. So the geologist was an office man and he'd never paddle a canoe, you know. And John had pulled that canoe all the way up. And he'd come to a point and I was told the geologist, now I'm going to investigate. Look, I'm going to go around here and see what I can find. And well, he was gone quite a bit, a long time. And the geologist got worried and he pulled out to the other side of the lake. He left the big canoe and he pulled out with a little coot the other side of the lake. And then got scared and he didn't go back the next day. Well, John, you know, he was lost. He waited quite a while and he couldn't figure out what the guy had done. And then he started, you know, about 20 or 30 miles from there, there was two lakes that came together. And there was a portage between the two lakes.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You know what a portage is?

speaker: Leo Guilfoy
words: Where they dragged the canoe over, one lake pours in the other. And he said, I'll get right there and wait and catch somebody portaging down. Well, he made that alright. Nothing had, he had nothing to eat, only mushrooms. And he made that place certainly just about gone. It took him quite a few days to get through the brush. And he was just about gone. And there was a guy, his name was Jack Hackness, he was coming, he was fat and he was coming in for his six months, grab a six months grub sake. He'd buy his six months. And he heard an animal there. Well, the wind was blowing on the wrong side and Hackness come down on this side of the lake, otherwise it had been on that side, on his portage of the lake. And he heard something in the brush when he was coming down that side, because the wind was blowing out with him. And he thought it was an animal. So he pulled a canoe up the shore and got his rifle out there. And there he was, John, and he was eaten up by black flies. So he got him into this little canoe and he had some flour with him. And he shot a couple of birds and made soup with his flour. And fed him just a little bit. And he made three portages with him into this big main lake.

speaker: Leo Guilfoy
words: And in the meantime, this bill here, right here from Bovill, and the other brother, same as he was, down here in Oregon there, Baker, Oregon, you know what that is?

speaker: Leo Guilfoy
words: In Baker, Oregon. They got together and they flew back to Duluth. They flew back to Duluth and got all ready to go on an expedition to hunt for them. And the Northwest Mounties, they were in Canada, they didn't see. And the Northwest Mounties helped them. And this one from Baker, he got an Indians, a couple of Indians, and some dogs in the canoe. And he went up by water. And Bill, he took the Northwest Mounties, flew him up there. They had the hydroplanes, you know, they landed on the lake. And in the meantime, this trap of bringing John down, there was an island in the middle of this lake. And this guy from Baker, going up that way, and the Indians said, oh no, that's just hackers coming around the island this way, with John. Of course he was laying down flat in the canoe. Oh, they said, no, don't, you know, I've stopped and seen him because he come down just about this time for a grub, for by his grub. Well, hackers didn't know who they was. They passed each other. They didn't pass by. One was just going around the edge of the island, he was coming down inside the island. And he kept on, he made a couple more porches down there. And there was a little mining shack, a young doctor there. And they got him into this young doctor. And the young doctor saw him right away. He said, we won't feed him. We'll just barely break him in. And he got him standing on his feet. And by that time, the Northwest Mounties had brought old Bill back. And then the other guy brought the Indians and the dogs back. And I'll forget now, Bill give this Jack Hackness, I think it was six or eight hundred dollars. And the other one from Baker gave him six hundred dollars. And that trapper said, my God, he said, that's the best trapper I ever had in my life. And the young doctor told him, you've done wonderful. Just feed them easy. And he'd shoot a bird, you know, and boil it. And he used a little flour for soup. He says, you'll shoot. But anyhow, they got him to Port, in Canada.

speaker: Leo Guilfoy
words: Port and by God, they killed him. Celebrate. Feed him up, you know, and he'd eat so much he got pneumonia and died.

speaker: Leo Guilfoy
words: This young doctor, he told him, what you feed him?

speaker: Leo Guilfoy
words: He's just out of school. He told the trapper, by God, he was on fire. He'd always be in good shape. He'd just boil this here, shoot some kind of a bird, you know, and boil it and make soup with the flour.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So whatever happened to this geologist? Did he get back all right himself?

speaker: Leo Guilfoy
words: Yeah, he got back home all right. No, of course, they boiled the hell out of him.

speaker: Leo Guilfoy
words: Not on his fault, he wasn't that kind of a man.

speaker: Leo Guilfoy
words: Crazy. Yeah.

speaker: Leo Guilfoy
words: It was Bill's brother, John. I bet his father. He was quite a man. He had four or five kids, five kids going to school at one time, college. She went to Lawrence College in Appleton, Wisconsin. It's a wonderful college. She majored in the violin. That's the thing, water under the bridge now.

speaker: Leo Guilfoy
words: Okay, now I'm just taking you away from the story. Oh, that's a good story.

speaker: Leo Guilfoy
words: Yeah, but then that wouldn't be nothing to do with later college.

speaker: Leo Guilfoy
words: But still, it's Bill's brother.

speaker: Leo Guilfoy
words: Yeah, his brother. A lot of these old timers down there, but I was down to our funeral, Finland around there. They all knew him. Said he'd come into the bank there one day and sold some of his claims, mining claims. He walked around with a quarter of a million dollars. Now, that money had trouble with his wife.

speaker: Leo Guilfoy
words: They had four or five going to college at the same time. That's quite an expense. Yeah, sure as heck is. I wish I had more of these old ones.

speaker: Leo Guilfoy
words: I'll give you an idea. All right, we had a guy in there, I can't think of his name now. He was what we call a car wacker. Now, I'll tell you what a car wacker is. All right, when they ship lots of poles and lots of timber, see. Well, he'd have to examine the load, see if they were all right, see if they would make it. If you loaded a load of poles, he'd examine after the load was loaded and see that the clearance was all right. For instance, an overhang like that, here's your flat car. There are bunks across there in these big long poles, you know. They'd have to stick right over the other car, see, because the flat car would only be about 40 feet long, 45. Well, it'd be down this one, there'd be a bunk across there, and they'd stick probably out eight or nine feet across. Well, you had to timber them up good, you know, and see that they had clearance when they swung round the curves, see that the pole would go swinging around the curve and sticking away out that way.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: They called them a cow wacker?

speaker: Leo Guilfoy
words: A car. A flat car, yeah. And then when a passenger train would come in, go along there and hit the wheels with a hammer. They don't do it anymore. And if they rang, sometimes there'd be a cracked wheel. And if they didn't ring, they used to ride in these big cities, you see a passenger train pull in and this guy would go and hit the wheel with his hammer, right along the train, both sides. And if they didn't ring, they were cracked. And right then, it takes that section out and puts it right in. Of course, I don't think they do it anymore, no. Well, anyhow, he had, but he drank a little, and he got to be Boy Scout leader. Not leader, but, you know, the guy that you probably know more than I do. Well, true Scout master. Ah, Scout master. You got to be Scout man. He was wonderful with him. Well, I used to help him. There was one big outfit, the Winton Lumber Company, and old Jimmy Sullivan. I told Jimmy, I said, now, you're leaving these cars out here, and I said, you'll all fix up and you won't be getting for quite a while. What's the matter with, don't let the Boy Scouts use them. Nice big dining rooms on them, you know, and plenty of room and all that. Well, I'd say, okay, well, I'd get them. I'd tell the Scout master, now, you'll have that for camping. They'll be leaving there for quite a while, and have to use that for camping. Oh, they had a wonderful time. And, buddy, they were a tough bunch. Oh, boy, they were a tough bunch. And then this time, I told you about this one where we fixed this old camp up in the woods. We got the dining room up there, and we fixed it all up, and we took the Boy Scouts out, and we kept them away where they could eat for quite a while. words: And he said, how much does he want for that cigar store Indian in the window?

speaker: Leo Guilfoy
words: Well, I told her what I thought he wanted for it. And she said, wait a minute, when he comes back, tell him he can have my husband, Sam, and his fishing rights and hunting rights go with him for $80.

speaker: Leo Guilfoy
words: Well, we stopped before you were starting to tell me all these prohibition, about prohibition, and about something bootlanging that was going on. I really wanted you to keep telling me some of that stuff. Yeah.

speaker: Leo Guilfoy
words: You know, it was really a fight around here, around there, you know. And it was a serious crime, really serious. And down at the county jail there, they had it full of bootleggers from Oropino and down Bovill and Southpik and those countries around there. Not much. And nowadays, years afterwards, one would meet the other one, and they'd all claim they went to the same college together. An old college friend of mine.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And they'd be pretty hard on a bootlegger when they came?

speaker: Leo Guilfoy
words: Nine months.

speaker: Leo Guilfoy
words: Of course, they were good here. Moscow was good.

speaker: Leo Guilfoy
words: Good jail.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did they get caught very often?

speaker: Leo Guilfoy
words: Well, they would do them nine months, you know.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, did they arrest a lot of people? Did many guys get caught?

speaker: Leo Guilfoy
words: Well, some of them never got caught.

speaker: Leo Guilfoy
words: Some of them never got caught, but others, you know, probably this deputy sheriff would blow in there and pretend he was a lumberjack with cockshaws on and everything. And the guy would tell them something.

speaker: Leo Guilfoy
words: The bootlegger.

speaker: Leo Guilfoy
words: Of course, the bootleggers had a lot of little places out there, ranches or something. And then they came in, the Greeks piled in there from Spokane.

speaker: Leo Guilfoy
words: I went out one time with the bunch from Bovill. Somebody come in and told us that Pat Malone was taking a bunch to search for an outfit to direct the car out of town. And we all went out there, you know, and Pat gave a lot of them flashlights to search around.

speaker: Leo Guilfoy
words: Maybe they shouldn't tell the rest of them. Oh, no, go ahead. It sounds like it's going to be a good story.

speaker: Leo Guilfoy
words: Well, I went out with another guy and we drove out there and he walked up on a windfall. Right at the end of the windfall, gee, there was a bunch of genuinely wine whiskey and about a five -gallon keg of wine. And these guys, they grabbed the whiskey and they didn't let the wine sticking out there. There were two Greeks in charge of that. They got them.

speaker: Leo Guilfoy
words: I heard that Pat wasn't very tough on the moonshiners because he was drinking so much himself.

speaker: Leo Guilfoy
words: He was a pretty good old solo. He'd been a cop in Duluth, Minnesota.

speaker: Leo Guilfoy
words: But you got quite a bit of dope that night. You've got quite a bit of dope on that tonight. On your tape there. Oh, yeah.

speaker: Leo Guilfoy
words: Well, I've been talking for an hour.

speaker: Leo Guilfoy
words: It was really funny those days.

speaker: Leo Guilfoy
words: Well, a fellow talking, he thinks of more to say, you know. One brings on another.

speaker: Leo Guilfoy
words: I was thinking of this story that you told him about the new teacher in Beauville. Oh, yeah. Tell me that one again. Yeah. Okay.

speaker: Leo Guilfoy
words: When school was over, there'd be lots of outsiders apply for a job for the next school teaching period. Some of them would quit and there'd be an opening for others. Well, they got letters from all over. They got one letter from some young girl. She was in a girl's school back east when he was finishing girl's school back east. And she got the job. And at that time, Mrs. David was in the boarding house. And we had a guy there, a long lumberjack, kind of looked after it. And for a few days, some of these outsiders had come in from different places. They had nowhere to board right away until a woman in the town found them out and picked them up and took them home to board. Well, this woman, this girl, the first chance for the first few days she had was in this boarding house. And they had pork and beans for dinner. Of course, she always used big, big words when she mentioned talking about anything, the teacher did. And the boarding boss, he shoved the pork and beans over to her and she took the pork and beans. And the pork was strong. And a few days later they had pork and beans again.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And the boarding boss said, lady, will you have some pork and beans?

speaker: Leo Guilfoy
words: She said, no, the pork is too astute. And he laughed and said, lady, the pork makes you astute, it's the beans. Now that's on there. And we all sort that out.

speaker: Leo Guilfoy
words: That's a good story. Good tie down.

speaker: Leo Guilfoy
words: And we'd be together.

speaker: Leo Guilfoy
words: Okay?

speaker: Leo Guilfoy
words: You'd go in and you'd buy yourself a beer. And you'd give them a twenty dollar bill. Well, this gentleman got the number of this ten dollar bill. He didn't go anywhere, he got the number out of it. And about two or three hours later he'd come in and planked down a dollar bill and buy himself a drink. He'd be carrying a pack of the laundry over his arm, you know, I used to. And he'd say, about ten, give him a change for a dollar. Oh, he says, you made a mistake. He said I was twenty. In the sack he said I didn't have anything to do with it, I had that bill on my hand, I wrote the number on my laundry here. And the earlier guy said, I don't know, call the manager. The manager's come over and here's the twenty dollar bill with that number that was on the man's laundry. Give him the change. And that was an old gang years ago. And not very long ago down in Orofino they pulled the same gang and they got by with three places. And the only reason they didn't get by on the last place, it had a long bar, it had a cash register at that end and a cash register in there. And twenty dollar man come in, you know. Oh, he said no, my God, God. And the guy looked at both, in both cash registers and it scared the guy. And they pulled it out, but they caught him. The cops caught him there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So good, so that they keep the men here?

speaker: Leo Guilfoy
words: Oh yeah, you bet. Keep the men in good shape, you know.

speaker: Leo Guilfoy
words: You keep them contented and that means a lot to the work they do outside.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Any of the jokes that you played on guys in Cedar Mill?

speaker: Leo Guilfoy
words: Because I read in the book, according to John Miller, that you like to play jokes. Yeah, I wish I could think of the right quick. Yeah, I did.

speaker: Leo Guilfoy
words: Well, I read about that one story about the guy that you were scaling that log and it was defective. Oh yeah, yeah.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You want to tell me that?

speaker: Leo Guilfoy
words: Because you haven't told me that one yet. Okay, he's dead too, that guy.

speaker: Leo Guilfoy
words: Well, a pole, see. Well, a pole has come in and I was a head scaler. And you had to classify the length, the width and everything like that. Classifications were six feet from the butt and the top classifications all come in together, you know. Oh, dozens and dozens of different classifications on even one pole. Well, this guy bought a bunch of poles, his own pole, see, and selling them to them. I'd always give him a good break, he knew that. And this pole, I sized it up and it looked kind of funny to me and I said, thank gosh, I'm positive that's rotten.

speaker: Leo Guilfoy
words: Right there.

speaker: Leo Guilfoy
words: And we come in and he said, no, I was not wrong with that. Well, I said, I think there is. Well, now he said, I'll tell you, you know, he said, if you cut that pole, you're going to pay for both ends. You're going to pay for the pole the way it is. Okay, he said, they take my check and they're going to pay it.

speaker: Leo Guilfoy
words: And I took, you don't know what an increment border is, do you?

speaker: Leo Guilfoy
words: No. Alright, it's like, alright, for instance, it's about that long and there's a hole there and there's another part of it. Anything but two handles. It fits in that hole and you snap this thing on.

speaker: Leo Guilfoy
words: And this is hollow in here and when you screw that in, the thread is on the outside. By the way, it was made in Sweden. And then you turn it down and it just will screw way down in there. As far as you turn it, if you only wanted it with three eighths, or, you side that pole up, I'd want to go by a hinge, you see. I'll screw it down eight inches and then it goes through, it's got a hole and you push a needle down in there. The length of this, there's room for a needle on the side. And then you pull it out and you pull, it's just side to side of a cigarette. And you can count all the rings on it. And it can show, I've had it a lot, thousands of times. And it shows, it'll show you the rings on it. If there's any rot, it'll show you the rot. It's called an increm -boi. I -N -C -R -E -M -E -N -T. And I'd have to tap hundreds of poles sometimes in a day, just three eighths, a half inches to give the penetration of the chrysote. Well, before he came, I'd like to screw it down into there, pull it out and I could see the rot. Well, he said, Leo, he said, that don't show any buzz. I said, it does. Something funny about it to me. Okay, he said, if you cut that pole, you'll pay me for the pole. Okay.

speaker: Leo Guilfoy
words: Now, I said, if I cut that pole and it's rotten, who's gonna cut the rest away till it stops being rotten?

speaker: Leo Guilfoy
words: By God, I would. I had a dandy. He said, my eyeball. Oh, okay. I called a couple of guys. Two saw -gangs, see. When I had marked it to cut, they'd cut it. Turn it into an old boy, rot like that. Now, I said, you will finish that, cut that every five feet. If it's rotten, man, he could have twice, I think. Five foot up. He ruined it for him, you know. The bottom part was just the piling and the top part was a small pole. I think the pole was about 50 -55 feet long. No, he couldn't. So, he started a gas station.

speaker: Leo Guilfoy
words: After I'd rocked, why not for my last time?

speaker: Leo Guilfoy
words: He started a gas station at Troy and, oh, he's always tickled me. I'd go through from Orofino, I would live there a little while, go into Moscow and I'd go through this place there and stop every time. His name was Helding Linneman. And I said to him one time, by God, I'm gonna quit coming here and buying gas from you.

speaker: Leo Guilfoy
words: I said, remember that pole?

speaker: Leo Guilfoy
words: And he laughed to be held. He said, I've been gypping you every time you come here on account of that pole. He hadn't. Well, I said, I figured you had. But I don't know what he had.

speaker: Leo Guilfoy
words: I found this guy.

speaker: Leo Guilfoy
words: He couldn't figure out, but it did look after him because it seemed so many of them, you know. Got a break in there when it was young or something. Cat face started in there.

speaker: Leo Guilfoy
words: Tell me about cat face.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What's cat face now?

speaker: Leo Guilfoy
words: A bruise on a pole when it's young.

speaker: Leo Guilfoy
words: It'll leave a bruise and then the bark will cover that over. It'll keep covering it over and there's rot in there, see. The rot will get in there and they don't want to take anything with a cat face on it. God, I used to know that the classification on the side of the pole, just like that. Most guys would have a big signboard up there. There's so much round here and at the top and you'd have to look down and I could stand right on one end. As soon as I had a guy on the other end, he just measured the top or if he measured the tail, he'd call the number back to me. I could remember them all just that quick. Go right on. One day there, it was maybe 10 or 11 hours, 1008 poles checked and remanufactured. Some of them, there were a wonderful bunch of poles. Mostly big long ones, 40s and 45s and longer. There were a wonderful bunch of pole trucks bringing them in and some of them come on flat guard. Then I'd have to mark any of the cuts, see, and then go back and reclassify them. 1008 in a day. This son of a gun that was on the other end of me, a young guy, his name was Wayne Smith. Oh, faster than that because all he knew was to number it. He had a tape that goes around him and he called the number back to me and then I'd have the number at the top of the bottle where he was at. Then I'd place the top with him, the size around the top, one, two, three or four sides on top. Then 1008 and then not counting these, we had a saw back. We had two saw gangs working. Three men in each side saw a gang. He was a sweet man with chalk and he'd go around and cut them up. You get that pretty fast. If you have a good memory, you get that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What else did you do besides scale on that cedar yard?

speaker: Leo Guilfoy
words: Well, I ran the treating plant for a long time.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You did them both at the same time, huh?

speaker: Leo Guilfoy
words: No.

speaker: Leo Guilfoy
words: No, one at a time. Yeah, I was getting plants and I could have been the job foreman but I didn't want to. Because it would make just as much the other way. I scaled all the poles that come in. The guy helped me. They had a big long runway and rails up to where the truck would dump them or where the flash car would dump them. Then the gang would roll them down and I'd be on the end of the tape and measure them and mark them with yellow chalk, yellow crane. Classified men would mark them. Of course, there was something wrong with them. I'd have to stop on them a little bit and measure them back and then go back after they sawed it. There were horses and rollers on this big long roadway. Rollers on the guy with the horse would pull them down and they'd dump them up on different sides to the size of the pole and all that. All the way down there. If they had 50s, 60s and all these flashes of them, they'd have a pile from each side. Then he'd just roll them up. He'd have some guys rolling them up for him. Then he'd turn around and probably have three or four poles to horse -pull them.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And they were on dead rollers, see?

speaker: Leo Guilfoy
words: A big four -foot roller. He'd pull them down there. But anyhow, this guy was so dead.

speaker: Leo Guilfoy
words: He, I don't think it was him after a while, a very serious guy. He'd come up and I'd have it marked. He'd throw the chain around it and then he'd hook up to the horse. He'd stand back and get up. It was a bit hard to start. Well, this time it was a very big pole. I think it was about 70. It was a good size. And I sized it up and I sawed five foot off the butt. And all I had to think was, mark its right length with the five foot still on it. And then I had the guy saw five foot off the butt. And it stood right there. It didn't move when he just sawed it off. And I just pushed it down the road a little bit and marked the length on the rest of it. So you only even push that piece back. And this guy came up there and he looked at it and he threw the chain around it and hooked it up five foot long. And he got dug down, had the horse dig down. Off they went, they had five foot chunk. God, he never forgot that. He never forgot that.

speaker: Leo Guilfoy
words: And it worked fast too. Probably three horses there. Three guys. One pulled away and the next one pulled on the rest.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you think he knew right away? Do you think he knew right away when he took off?

speaker: Leo Guilfoy
words: Oh, well, little ways he had because he never looked back. And when he noticed the horse wasn't doing anything, hardly pulling it.

speaker: Leo Guilfoy
words: I used to go down and see him. He moved down to Clarkson. God, he loved to see me come in. He sure loved to see me. His wife lives there now alone. He sure loved to see me come in. We were always laughing in the polly stunts.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What about that old vat in the treating plant?

speaker: Leo Guilfoy
words: It seemed to me like from what I read about that in John Miller's book, that was a pretty dangerous place. Well, they had these great big tanks, you know, twelve foot deep.

speaker: Leo Guilfoy
words: And the whole of Colorado poles. They'd stand them up to one end. And the crew thought I'd have had lots of times up to two hundred and sixty degrees Fahrenheit. You know, boiling's 212. And many a time, you know, there was no hook in them. They had to go on top of the road after you pumped them out. Had to watch them fall in. And I wouldn't let them work for full on the pole until I got every drop of boiling crease out of it. I was like, wait, because if they drip anyhow on them, crease or that son of gun burns you.

speaker: Leo Guilfoy
words: Or anything.

speaker: Leo Guilfoy
words: Well, I forgot a lot of them jokes I used to play on them. I forgot them. I told you something about old man Miller, you know. He had a peg leg.

speaker: Leo Guilfoy
words: No, he didn't tell me that one. Okay, and Johnny didn't want that.

speaker: Leo Guilfoy
words: Well, when I was running the plant, old man Miller, they were kind of up against him. And I'd give him a job firing after we was all laid fire. If we had to pump out in the morning, we had to have the steam and all that. Well, this night it had snowed to be hell. And he had to take the wheel by and get wood, see. And I'd come down in the middle. I couldn't, I thought I was seeing things. There'd be two holes, a footprint and one hole, and then a straight line.

speaker: Leo Guilfoy
words: God, I'd tear my head open.

speaker: Leo Guilfoy
words: That's the way it'd be right along there. There'd be two holes, then a footprint and one hole, then a straight hole. After all, there'd be another couple of straight holes down, then a footprint. And I got there. It took me a long time to figure. It was old peg leg Miller with the wheel by him. He'd rest the wheel by him. He had legs on it, you know. And they'd make two holes. When he started off, his foot on this side and his peg leg on that side. And then the wheel would make a straight line way down. God.

speaker: Leo Guilfoy
words: That must have been a hard one to figure out. Yeah, sure.

speaker: Leo Guilfoy
words: I thought I was seeing things.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How did this cat face, what was his name? Cat face Ed?

speaker: Leo Guilfoy
words: Cat face Ed, yeah.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How'd he get his name?

speaker: Leo Guilfoy
words: I'd give it to him.

speaker: Leo Guilfoy
words: I'd go out in the lobby and a lot of the polls would come in with a cat face on them. I'd have to turn them over and they'd have spotted that cat face and that cut it, you know. Well, you mean you always had to turn it over. You didn't find him on time. No, they'd turn them over so you couldn't see him. You know, it's hard to turn a big poll over in the woods.

speaker: Leo Guilfoy
words: Real hard.

speaker: Leo Guilfoy
words: With people, you know. And all the time I don't remember having a broken poll. That'll help this guy now. He's quite a lager now. He's not very old either. Well, I was over to party with him here. Phil, him and his wife gave parties for their best customers, see. And this Gould, he showed lots of business to Phil. All these different truckers and a couple of Sawmills down around the Europeano and all that country. So they invited him up to a big meal once in a while. Well, we was putting out a big bunch of polls for the REA.

speaker: Leo Guilfoy
words: You know what that is?

speaker: Leo Guilfoy
words: Rural Electrification. That came in right after Roosevelt. Rural Electrification. And they had government engineers. Well, they'd give you how to frame the polls. See, some of them was framed down on me and some of them would be framed hip like that. And this REA had a system of their own and so many holes this way for cables to hold them here and there, you know. And again, where to put the cross arm, that machine needed to do all that. Well, they had this school, this dad, he's retired now. His dad hauled some of these logs down to the Clearwater Valley down in there around the Europeano and all that country. And they had, those engineers would make mistakes and change them. Well, when you brought a hole in, you had to plug it with a hot crystal plug. Well, if the bolt was going, of course the bolts would go in the field without the... So, a lot of these holes in the field, so I got a big bunch of plugs and I treated them. I put them in and I boiled them in with the rest of the poles.

speaker: Leo Guilfoy
words: And I forget if it was John Gould or anyhow, or somebody working for Gould. When he got ready to haul this load of poles, I threw this up on the pole pile, you know, on with the poles. And he said to me once, ah, it's a dead cat. And I'd be God damn if he didn't throw it up. God, I'm not going to throw it up.

speaker: Leo Guilfoy
words: And his engineers went and said, didn't he give you any plugs?

speaker: Leo Guilfoy
words: No.

speaker: Leo Guilfoy
words: Didn't he give you any at all?

speaker: Leo Guilfoy
words: No. No. He took me, you know, I didn't know I was kidding him.

speaker: Leo Guilfoy
words: And he'd throw this cat sack of plugs. It's about that long, you know. That's what happens when you joke people. Yeah, something like that happens to me too. People think I'm serious, but I'm just kidding them.

speaker: Leo Guilfoy
words: And he threw it off. Jesus, I had to search for hours to find it. I searched for hours to find it. Another time there, they put a bunch of poles down on some place and the trucker came back and he told me, he says, hey, those guys, the government officers set the poles up. But he said this one man, he told me where it was. He said, you know, Woodpecker, if any boy was in the pole, he'd be, you see. Not bad. And I said, no, God, none of those poles were up. I know there was. Well, he told me where it was. It was about 20 miles out there, 15 or 20 miles. So I went out there and I came with the bass. He didn't have, buh buh buh buh buh buh buh buh. Well, they hadn't got any machinery. No wires onto it, cross arms or anything. They hadn't put that on yet. So I told them, I said, you ought to pull that out. I saw it. I pulled it out and saw it with not a stain of a worm. Of course, the company paid for it. They'd rather do that than have it wormy. I pulled it out and they couldn't figure out why they would. Woodpecker was pecking in there. They don't peck unless they listen and then peck.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Have you ever figured?

speaker: Leo Guilfoy
words: No, neither did I. I figured it would be a nutty.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What's all I told you?

speaker: Leo Guilfoy
words: You can get him now. Next time you get him, shoot him. Yeah, a nutty woodpecker. I'll tell you how nutty they are. We had a great big boiler, you know, to heat this creosote. Big smoke stacks you can wave up and cable down. And I seen one up there pecking on that. On this iron smoke stack. Put my boot like that.

speaker: Leo Guilfoy
words: By a woodpecker. Yeah, a woodpecker pecking on him. And I called him up and said, take a look at this.

speaker: Leo Guilfoy
words: Ah, why don't I say that?

speaker: Leo Guilfoy
words: That son of a bitch was pecking on that pole around Avon.

speaker: Leo Guilfoy
words: Same.

speaker: Leo Guilfoy
words: Now, the pole business has gone to hell now. Because where they buy poles, some of these guys, dig companies and all that, would rather cut the poles up into lumber. They get more out of it. Like I was telling you about siding and all that. The beautiful cedar siding in Avon, you know, in these homes. Nobody would want to get so much for a pole in the woods. Now I'll take one of them and saw them up into this stuff here with three or four hundred dollars a thousand.

speaker: Leo Guilfoy
words: And they like that nutty stuff.
